verses.book: Letters of St. Chrysostom to Olympias.
verses.text: INTRODUCTION TO THE LETTERS TO OLYMPIAS.

verses.book: Letters of St. Chrysostom to Olympias.
verses.text: The deaconess Olympias to whom seventeen of Chrysostom’s extant letters are addressed was the most eminent of his female friends.

verses.book: Letters of St. Chrysostom to Olympias.
verses.text: She belonged to a Pagan family of high rank, and was born about 368.

verses.book: Letters of St. Chrysostom to Olympias.
verses.text: Her father Seleucus who was a count of the Empire died when she was a young girl and she was brought up under the guardianship of an uncle Procopius, who has a devout Christian and a friend of Gregory of Nazianzus.

verses.book: Letters of St. Chrysostom to Olympias.
verses.text: Gregory took great interest in her, speaking of her in his letters as “his own Olympias” and delighting to be addressed by her as “father.”

verses.book: Letters of St. Chrysostom to Olympias.
verses.text: Her governess Theodosia, sister of St. Amphilochius of Iconium, was a woman whom Gregory exhorted her to imitate as the very pattern of Christian goodness.

verses.book: Letters of St. Chrysostom to Olympias.
verses.text: The orphan girl had great personal beauty, and was the heiress of a large fortune.

verses.book: Letters of St. Chrysostom to Olympias.
verses.text: Naturally therefore she had many suitors, and in 384 at the age of sixteen she was wedded to Nebridius, a young man of high rank and irreproachable character.

verses.book: Letters of St. Chrysostom to Olympias.
verses.text: The marriage however does not seem to have been a happy one, and perhaps in this fact as well as in the death of her husband about two years after their union, Olympias saw a divine intimation that she should not entangle herself again in the worldly cares and anxieties incident to married life.

verses.book: Letters of St. Chrysostom to Olympias.
verses.text: The Emperor Theodosius wished to unite her to a young Spaniard, Elpidius, a kinsman of his own, and irritated by her refusal, ordered her property to be confiscated until she should have attained her thirtieth year, unless she consented to the proposed union.

verses.book: Letters of St. Chrysostom to Olympias.
verses.text: Olympias however remained inflexible and in a letter of dignified sarcasm thanked the Emperor for relieving her from a heavy burden.

verses.book: Letters of St. Chrysostom to Olympias.
verses.text: “He could not have conferred a greater blessing upon her unless he had ordered her wealth to be bestowed upon the Churches and the poor.”

verses.book: Letters of St. Chrysostom to Olympias.
verses.text: Theodosius perceiving the uselessness, if not regretting the injustice, of his harsh decree, cancelled it, and left her in the undisturbed enjoyment of her property.

verses.book: Letters of St. Chrysostom to Olympias.
verses.text: Henceforward her time and wealth were devoted to the service of religion.

verses.book: Letters of St. Chrysostom to Olympias.
verses.text: She ministered to the necessities of the sick and poor, and supported the work of the Church in Greece, Asia Minor and Syria with such lavish donations, not only of her money but of her land, that even Chrysostom, who might be called the great preacher of almsgiving, warned her against indiscriminate liberality, reminding her that as her wealth was a trust committed to her by God she ought to be discreet in the management of it.

verses.book: Letters of St. Chrysostom to Olympias.
verses.text: This salutary advice gained him the ill-will of many avaricious bishops and clergy who had profited, or hoped to profit, by her gifts.

verses.book: Letters of St. Chrysostom to Olympias.
verses.text: She in her turn requited the Archbishop for his spiritual care by many little feminine attentions to his bodily wants, especially by seeing that he was supplied with wholesome food, and did not overstrain his feeble constitution by a too rigid abstinence.

verses.book: Letters of St. Chrysostom to Olympias.
verses.text: She herself however practised the most austere asceticism, renouncing the luxury of the bath, wearing none but old coarse clothing, and subjecting herself to severe restrictions in respect of food and sleep.

verses.book: Letters of St. Chrysostom to Olympias.
verses.text: After the expulsion of Chrysostom from Constantinople 404, through the intrigues of his enemies, Olympias suffered much from the persecution to which all his followers were subjected.

verses.book: Letters of St. Chrysostom to Olympias.
verses.text: She was accused of having been concerned in causing the fire which broke out immediately after his departure, and destroyed the Cathedral Church and the Senate House.

verses.book: Letters of St. Chrysostom to Olympias.
verses.text: Her intrepid demeanour before the præfect who tried in vain to frighten her into a confession of guilt, or induce her to acknowledge Arsacius who had been intruded into the See by an arbitrary exercise of imperial power, excited general admiration; and the tidings of her fortitude were a great consolation to the exiled archbishop in the midst of much bodily suffering, and mental distress.

verses.book: Letters of St. Chrysostom to Olympias.
verses.text: It is not quite certain whether she was driven from Constantinople or voluntarily retired from it; nor have we any definite information concerning the remainder of her life.
